title: Khandvi
description: A delicate Gujarati snack of rolled gram-flour batter, finished with a mustard-seed tempering. The tricky part is the batter — the 'plate test' is your guide to knowing when it's ready.

steps.title: Make the buttermilk
steps.content: In a bowl, whisk the {i2} with {i3} until smooth and well combined.
steps.why: This thin, smooth buttermilk is the base of the batter — getting it lump-free here makes everything easier later.

steps.title: Add the spices
steps.content: Stir in the {i4}, {i5}, {i6}, and {i7}. If your yogurt isn't very sour, add a squeeze of lemon juice for tang.
steps.why: The 1:3 ratio of besan to buttermilk is what gives khandvi its texture — keep that proportion even if you scale the recipe.

steps.title: Whisk in the besan
steps.content: Add the {i1} and whisk thoroughly until the batter is completely smooth, with no lumps.
steps.why: Any lumps now become hard bits later. Keep whisking — a hand whisk or beater both work.

steps.title: Prep your rolling surface
steps.content: Lightly grease large plates, trays, or a clean countertop with oil. Also mix the {i8} and {i9} together in a small bowl for the filling.
steps.why: The cooked batter sets fast, so have your surface and filling ready before you start cooking — there is no pause once it's done.

steps.title: Cook the batter
steps.content: Pour the batter into a saucepan over the lowest heat. Stir constantly as it heats, thickens, and eventually leaves the sides of the pan.
steps.why: Low heat and constant stirring prevent lumps. This usually takes around 15-17 minutes — resist the urge to raise the heat to speed it up.

steps.title: Do the plate test
steps.content: Spread a small spoonful of batter thinly on a plate, let it cool for a few seconds, then try to roll it. If it rolls smoothly without sticking, the batter is ready. If it tears or stays pasty, cook a little longer and test again.
steps.why: This is the single most important step. Undercooked batter won't roll; overcooked batter gets too thick to spread thinly.

steps.title: Spread it thin
steps.content: Working quickly while the batter is still hot, pour about ½ to ⅔ cup onto a greased plate and spread it into a thin, even layer with a spatula. Repeat with the remaining batter on your other plates.
steps.why: Speed is everything here — the batter thickens as it cools, so a delay means thicker, lumpier layers.

steps.title: Add the filling
steps.content: Once each spread layer has cooled slightly, sprinkle it lightly and evenly with the coconut-coriander mixture.
steps.why: This stuffed style is a regional variation — if you'd rather skip it, just garnish the rolls with coconut and coriander after tempering instead.

steps.title: Cut and roll
steps.content: Cut the cooled batter into even strips, then gently roll each strip into a tight spiral. Arrange the rolls on a serving plate, packed closely together.
steps.why: If a strip tears instead of rolling, that section was probably spread too thick — just roll it as best you can, it will still taste good.

steps.title: Make the tempering
steps.content: Heat the {i10} in a small pan. Add the {i12} and let them crackle, then add the {i11} and {i14} and fry for a few seconds. Finally stir in the {i13} until they crackle and change color.
steps.why: This spiced oil, called a "tempering" or "tadka", is poured over the dish at the end for a final hit of aroma and crunch.

steps.title: Finish and serve
steps.content: Pour the hot tempering, oil and all, evenly over the khandvi rolls. Garnish with extra coconut and coriander if you like, and serve with coriander chutney.

title: Vegetable Green Thai Curry
description: An authentic homemade Thai green curry with freshly made paste, coconut milk and crunchy vegetables. The fresh paste is what makes it special.

steps.title: Toast the spices
steps.content: Lightly toast {i1} and {i2} in a dry pan for about 1 minute until fragrant.
steps.why: Toasting wakes up the essential oils in the spices, giving the paste a deeper, more complex flavour.

steps.title: Grind the spices
steps.content: Transfer the toasted seeds and {i3} to a mortar and pestle (or grinder) and grind into a fine powder.

steps.title: Make the paste
steps.content: Add {i4}, {i5}, {i6}, {i7}, {i8}, {i9}, {i10}, {i11}, {i12}, and {i13} to the mortar. Pound for 10–15 minutes (or grind for 1–2 minutes) until a fine, bright green paste forms.
steps.why: A mortar and pestle bruises the fibres rather than cutting them — it gives a more aromatic paste. A blender is fine if you're short on time.

steps.title: Reduce the coconut milk
steps.content: Pour {i14} into a wide pan and cook on high heat for 2–3 minutes until it thickens. No oil needed.
steps.why: Reducing a small amount of coconut milk first concentrates the fat so the paste fries rather than boils — this is the key step most people skip.

steps.title: Fry the paste
steps.content: Add 3–4 tbsp of your fresh paste to the thickened coconut milk. Cook for 3–4 minutes, stirring, until the paste darkens and the oil separates from the coconut milk.
steps.why: The oil splitting ("cracking") means the paste is cooked through and the raw flavour is gone — don't skip this step.

steps.title: Add remaining coconut milk
steps.content: Pour in {i15} and stir well to combine, making sure there are no lumps of paste.

steps.title: Cook the hard vegetables
steps.content: Add {i16} first and boil for 7–8 minutes.
steps.why: Baby corn takes the longest to cook — adding it first means everything finishes at the same time.

steps.title: Add remaining vegetables
steps.content: Add {i17}, {i18}, {i19}, {i20}, {i21}, {i22}, and salt to taste. Cook for 3–4 minutes — the vegetables should stay crunchy and hold their colour.
steps.why: Overcooking kills both the colour and the crunch. Pull the pan off the moment the vegetables are just tender.

steps.title: Final infusion
steps.content: Add {i23} (torn slightly), {i24}, and {i25}. Cook for just 2 more minutes.
steps.why: The lime leaves and basil are fragile — too much heat and their aroma is lost. Two minutes is enough to infuse.

steps.title: Serve
steps.content: Serve hot with jasmine rice.

title: Veg Dum Biryani
description: A layered, aromatic biryani with parboiled basmati rice and spiced vegetables, slow-cooked on dum for perfectly separated, flavourful grains.

steps.title: Soak the rice
steps.content: Wash {i1} thoroughly in cold water until the water runs clear. Soak in fresh water for at least 30 minutes, then drain.
steps.why: Soaking hydrates the grains so they elongate fully during cooking and stay separate — never skip this step for biryani.

steps.title: Soak the saffron
steps.content: Crush {i13} lightly and soak in {i14}. Set aside — it will deepen in colour and flavour as it sits.

steps.title: Prep the vegetables
steps.content: Chop {i2}, {i3}, {i4}, and {i6} into even, medium-sized pieces. Keep {i5} aside. Uniform size ensures everything cooks at the same rate.

steps.title: Marinate the vegetables
steps.content: In a large bowl, mix together {i7}, {i8}, half of {i9}, half of {i10}, {i11}, {i21}, {i22}, {i23}, and {i24}. Add all the vegetables and mix well. Leave to marinate for at least 20 minutes.
steps.why: The yogurt marinade tenderises the vegetables and builds the flavour of the base gravy — the longer it marinates, the better.

steps.title: Cook the base gravy
steps.content: Heat {i25} in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add the marinated vegetables and cook, stirring occasionally, for 8–10 minutes until the gravy thickens and the oil begins to separate.
steps.why: You want the gravy thick enough to support the rice layers without making them soggy.

steps.title: Parboil the rice
steps.content: Bring a large pot of water to a boil. Add {i15}, {i16}, {i17}, {i18}, {i19}, {i20}, and salt generously. Add the soaked rice and cook until it is 70–80% done (grains should still have a slight bite). Drain immediately.
steps.why: Parboiling to exactly 70–80% is the most important step — the rice finishes on dum. Overcooked rice here means mushy biryani.

steps.title: Layer the biryani
steps.content: Spread the vegetable gravy evenly across the bottom of a heavy pot. Layer the parboiled rice on top. Drizzle the saffron milk, {i12}, and scatter the remaining {i9} and {i10} over the rice.
steps.why: Layering from the bottom up — gravy first, then rice, then aromatics on top — gives you the classic streaked, aromatic biryani look when you serve it.

steps.title: Seal and cook on dum
steps.content: Cover the pot tightly with a lid (seal the edges with dough or foil if needed). Cook on high heat for 2 minutes, then reduce to the lowest possible heat and cook for 15–20 minutes.
steps.why: Dum cooking traps all the steam inside — this is what mingles the aromas and finishes the rice perfectly. The dough seal is traditional; foil works fine.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.content: Turn off the heat and let the biryani rest undisturbed for 5 minutes. Then open the lid and gently fluff from the sides with a wide spatula, keeping the layers as intact as possible. Serve hot with raita.
steps.why: Resting lets the steam redistribute. Always fluff from the sides — stirring from the top breaks the grains and collapses the layers.

title: Pav Bhaji (from scratch)
description: Mumbai street-style pav bhaji — a buttery mashed-vegetable curry with homemade pav bhaji masala and a final chilli-garlic tadka, served with soft buttered pav.

ingredients.name: Kashmiri red chillies, deseeded & soaked (for paste)

steps.title: Make the pav bhaji masala
steps.content: Dry-roast the whole spices (coriander, cumin, cloves, star anise, black cardamom, cinnamon, bay leaves, Kashmiri chillies, fennel) until fragrant, then grind with the kasuri methi, turmeric, dry ginger, hing, black salt, aamchur and salt into a fine powder.
steps.why: Dry-roasting wakes up the spices' oils. This makes far more than one batch needs — store the rest in a jar for next time.

steps.title: Make the chilli & garlic pastes
steps.content: Grind the soaked Kashmiri chillies with a little water into a fine, runny paste. Separately grind the garlic with water into a thin, watery paste.
steps.why: Keep both pastes loose and runny so they blend smoothly into the bhaji without leaving raw bits.

steps.title: Boil the vegetables
steps.content: Boil the cauliflower florets and green peas with a pinch of turmeric and salt until soft. Drain and set aside.
steps.why: Pre-boiling means they mash down into the bhaji easily later.

steps.title: Start the base
steps.content: On a wide tawa or pan, melt the butter with the oil. Crackle the cumin, then cook the onions until pinkish. Add the capsicum and cook on high for 2–3 minutes.

steps.title: Add veggies & spices, then mash
steps.content: Add the boiled veg, mashed potatoes, tomatoes, grated beetroot, salt, pav bhaji masala, the powdered spices and green chilli paste. Mash and break everything down with a masher.
steps.why: The grated beetroot gives that classic deep-red colour without piling on extra chilli heat.

steps.title: Add pastes & simmer
steps.content: Add the prepared garlic and red chilli pastes with 1–2 cups of hot water. Keep the flame low and mash until almost smooth, adding more hot water to reach the consistency you like.
steps.why: Street-style bhaji is slightly thin — loosen it with hot water to taste. This stage takes 15–20 minutes.

steps.title: Make the tadka
steps.content: In a small pan, heat the butter, add a pinch of cumin, then the red chilli paste, garlic paste, pav bhaji masala and garam masala. Cook 1–2 minutes, then stir the whole tadka into the simmering bhaji.
steps.why: This final spiced butter is what gives restaurant pav bhaji its glossy red finish and punch.

steps.title: Finish & serve
steps.content: Stir in chopped coriander, a knob of butter and the lemon juice. Cook 2–3 minutes more. Serve hot with soft buttered pav.
